preamble: The bilabial nasal consonant /m/ can undergo a process of vocalization, transforming into the close front unrounded vowel /i/ under specific phonetic conditions. This type of shift, where a consonant becomes a vowel, is a form of extreme lenition, often triggered by the phonetic environment.
phoneticEffects: Vocalization, lenition, assimilation (specifically, a nasal consonant assimilating to a vocalic quality and frontness, often triggered by a following sibilant or fricative)

examples.note: In Montana Salish, /m/ changes to [i] before /s/ and sometimes other fricatives. This rule is morphologized, occurring even when certain s-less morphemes intervene between the nasal and the triggering /s/.

sources: Thomason, S. G. (2010). Dorsal developments in Montana Salish. In S. Morey & M. W. Post (Eds.), Language and Culture in Northeast India and Beyond. ANU Press.
